title: About Me
body: I’m Han-yu Wang. You can call me Henry. I was born in Taiwan , grew up in Shanghai , am studying in Hong Kong , and have also spent meaningful stretches of time in Melbourne and around Hastings . I’m a fourth-year student majoring in Applied Artificial Intelligence at the University of Hong Kong . A lot of my academic work is about learning. I look at how people learn from experience, how AI systems pick up patterns, and why both can look sensible from the outside while something more complicated is happening underneath. For the academic and professional record in one place, visit my CV → 多良 · 太麻里 · 台東 Duoliang · Taimali · Taitung I also like building things around ideas. Some are practical tools, some are interactive explanations, and some are small systems that make scattered material easier to search, ask about, or play with. The Projects page collects the fuller record, from company and lab work to coursework, side projects, and a few older things from secondary school. This website has slowly become part of the same habit, working as a portfolio, a personal archive, and a small playground. Use the magnifying glass to ask about anything on the site.

title: On a More Personal Level
body: I like traveling, but not only as collecting places. What stays with me is usually more specific, like a ferry crossing Sydney Harbour at sunset, a seaside road in Taitung, the Acropolis in the heat, the Mediterranean water, or ordinary walks near the sea after a dense day. Melbourne mattered differently too, because it was not just an exchange semester. It became part of my family life and daily rhythm for a while. Click the compass to explore places that have stayed with me. When I’m not working, you’ll often find me by the water. In New Zealand it was Napier’s oceanside. In Hong Kong it is often Kennedy Town Praya. Elsewhere it may just be a ferry, a harbour, or a long walk along the coast. The sea resets me somehow. For the less formal side, Fragments collects some of my looser thoughts and small life pieces, from AI, memory, and language to travel, family, food, and ordinary moments that stayed with me.

title: My Work Experience
body: A few of the roles that shaped how I think about technology, and about what it takes to turn an idea into something people actually use.

July 2026 - December 2026 Cloud, Data & AI Consulting Intern PricewaterhouseCoopers, Hong Kong I'm a Cloud, Data & AI Consulting Intern at PwC Hong Kong, a natural next step after my earlier work in AI, data, and digital transformation. What interests me most is seeing how technical ideas play out in consulting settings, especially when they need to be explained clearly and connected to business decisions. The work is sharpening how I think from both sides: the technical side, and the side of the people and organizations actually using the technology.

May 2024 - Present Part-time Student Research Assistant Speech, Language, and Reading Lab, HKU, Hong Kong This role is closely related to a question I have been thinking about for a long time: how do people learn, remember, and gradually change through experience? I work with experimental data and computational models to study learning behaviour over time. What interests me most is not only whether a model performs well, but whether it captures something real about the way people learn. The work has pulled me further into the space between cognitive science and AI, especially questions around memory, structure, and learning processes. August 2024 - December 2024 Digital Transformation Intern Hongkong and Shanghai Banking Corporation Limited, Hong Kong My internship at HSBC was the first time I saw how much of digital transformation is really about structure before it is about technology. I worked on product and distribution mapping across different markets, where the information often came in fragments and rarely in the same shape twice. A surprising amount of the work was simply making things comparable. I was also involved in a UI transformation project. Business fields, scenarios, and UI components all had to be organised into something much clearer than the original mix of documents, spreadsheets, and conversations. Part of my work there became an Excel and VBA-based workflow, which ended up being one of the more satisfying things I built that year. Alongside that, I sat in on a number of AI use case discussions in personal banking. That was probably where I became more cautious, in a good way, about AI. The interesting question was rarely whether something could be done with AI, but whether it made sense in context, given the workflow, the risk, and the people still needed around it. title: Research I've Been Part Of
body: My research sits where cognitive science meets AI. I want to understand the principles behind how a system, human or artificial, learns from experience, reasons, and decides, and I study them from both sides at once. On the human side, I ask how the mind turns experience into structure: how we pick up patterns, when that learning hardens into an explicit rule rather than a feel for the examples, and what a learning curve or a reaction time can actually tell us about the process underneath. I write these processes down as computational models and test them against behavior, some of it as a part-time research assistant at HKU's SLR Lab (https://slrlab.edu.hku.hk/), with Dr. Stephen M. K. Lee. On the machine side, I put the same questions to AI systems, language models in particular, with interpretability as the through-line: not just what a model does, but what it represents and how it computes that inside, turning opaque behavior into mechanisms we can name and reason about. I work through model internals and carefully designed behavioral tests, sometimes putting models and people through the very same task. For me these are not two fields but one question asked twice. The longer game is an account of learning and reasoning precise enough to hold for a person and a model alike, where what we learn about minds helps us read what is going on inside machines, and what we find inside machines feeds back into how we understand ourselves. The projects below are early steps in that direction. Human learning and computational cognition Computational models of how people learn, represent, and generalize. September 2025 – June 2026

title: Insurance Product Configuration Engine
body: An engine that turns an insurance product into the exact app screens it needs Associated with HSBC (https://www.hsbc.com/) On HSBC's insurance digital-transformation team, I tackled a quietly painful problem: across dozens of insurance products and a dozen markets, which app screens should each product actually show? Those rules lived scattered across business, design, and engineering, and kept drifting out of sync. I encoded them into one structure: three linked libraries plus a decision table, driven by an Excel and VBA engine that turns a product's specification into the exact set of UI components it needs, automatically. It went through four versions and shipped with a built-in tutorial so the team could keep running it after I left. Because the real logic is dense, I rebuilt a small, playable version you can drive in your browser, with a walkthrough that explains the problem before it hands you the controls: Try the interactive walkthrough → July 2024 - April 2025

body: A Full-Stack Adaptive Learning Portal with Vue, Flask, and Firestore Associated with PDS Lab (https://psym-ds.github.io/) Working with Prof. Jinsong Chen, I built an AI-powered education portal from scratch with Vue.js, Flask, and Firestore. title: Interactive Scrollytelling Deck
body: A Schema-Driven Scrollytelling Engine with a Pluggable D3 Chart Library This started as a course project: I had a data-heavy topic to present and wanted to tell it as a story that unfolds while you scroll, not a wall of charts. It grew well past the assignment into a reusable scrollytelling engine. Every scene is declared in a single schema, so text, tables, cards, and charts share consistent layout and reveal timing, with a registry mapping each figure to a D3 chart builder: line, ridge, scatter, timeline, heatmap, geo maps, Sankey, and chord. A background manager streams a different looping video per section, and it all runs as plain ES modules with no build step, so it hosts anywhere static. The live demo below is the course deck itself, built entirely on the engine. Walkthrough: See how the engine works → Live demo: henryw.me/CCGL9074 (https://www.henryw.me/CCGL9074/) September 2025 – December 2025

title: Stride Support
body: Customer Support Copilot with MCP Tools and Local LLM Stride Support is a customer support copilot that runs entirely on-device. A FastAPI router speaks the OpenAI-compatible API and orchestrates five MCP (Model Context Protocol) tool servers (catalog search, order lookups, complaints, recommendations, and policy QA) over a local LLM via LM Studio. The React UI lets the user pick from ongoing or past orders, and the router injects that context into every prompt so the model stays grounded in what the customer actually has. A semantic similarity gate blocks off-topic queries, and a fine-tuned mBERT classifier tags sentiment in real time so the model can adjust tone. Project page: github.com/backprop07/STAT4011 (https://github.com/backprop07/STAT4011) On my own time Side projects, built outside work and class. February 2026 – Present

title: Congentra
body: AI-Native Oral Verification Platform for Assignment Understanding I'm building Congentra, a platform that helps teachers check whether a student actually understands the work they handed in and can defend it. Instead of trying to detect AI use, it reads the assignment and the submission, generates a few targeted oral questions, collects the student's spoken answers on their own time, and packages everything into one evidence file a reviewer can skim. The whole thing is built around the reviewer: less time spent, and more to actually go on when making a call. February 2025 - June 2025

title: Lexaday
body: A Daily Vocabulary Coach with Generative Mnemonics and Spaced Review Lexaday is a small learning system that turns word roots into a daily email habit. Each run chooses a new root, generates a short motivational hook, creates example words and sentences, attaches a visual mnemonic, and brings back older roots on a growing review interval. The project keeps per-user progress in JSON, commits generated images and state through GitHub Actions, and uses Hugging Face models for both language and image generation so the email feels more like a personalized lesson than a static flashcard. Walkthrough: See how Lexaday works → Project repo: github.com/henryhyw/lexaday (https://github.com/henryhyw/lexaday) February 2025 - June 2025

The useful part is the last mile: instead of a raw scrape, the output is a clean Gmail digest grouped by brand, with product images, original and sale prices, discount percentages, and direct links ready to act on. title: Insurance Product Configuration Engine
body: Here's something you've done a hundred times: packing for a trip . The kind of trip decides what goes in the bag. Say "beach" and you need swimwear; add "water sports" and a snorkel earns its place; say "ski" and it's a completely different bag. You're running a tiny rules engine in your head, turning a few answers into the right list. At HSBC I built exactly that engine, for a much harder version of the same problem. Below is a small, fully playable version. It takes a couple of minutes, and the second half hands you the controls.

title: First, the real problem
body: Picture one banking app that sells many kinds of insurance. Every product needs a different set of screens : a travel policy asks for trip dates, a car policy asks about your vehicle, and some questions only appear once an earlier answer unlocks them. Now multiply that by dozens of products across a dozen markets. The rules for "which screen shows when" lived scattered in people's heads. Business knew the requirements, designers knew the screens, developers wired it together, and over time the three drifted out of sync. There was no single place you could point to and say this is how a product becomes a set of screens.

title: The idea: stop mixing three questions
body: I split the mess into three small libraries, each owned by the team that actually knows the answer: Spec What does the business need to know? The questions. Components What has the team actually built? The building blocks. Config Small rule tables linking the two: given these answers, use these blocks. Because they're separate, business can add a question, design can add a block, and neither breaks the other. Those rule tables are where the magic happens, and they're what you'll drive next.

title: Now you try it
body: Same machine, on the friendly example: you're packing for a trip. Answer the questions, watch each little rule table pick a winner, then watch the bag fill with exactly the right things. 1 Tell us about the trip ↳ Notice the follow-up question appears only when it's relevant. In the real tool that condition is written right into the field's name, so the form reshapes itself as you fill it in. 2 Each decision gets its own rule table Every table works the same way: keep the columns whose conditions all match your answers, and the most specific one on the right wins. ALL is a wildcard that matches anything. Your answers (inputs) What to pack (outputs) 1"> Your answers match columns; the most specific, “ ” , wins. Your answers land on “ ” . 3 The bag packs itself Packing list Just the essentials for this trip. Same answers in, the same list out, every time. Each table contributes its part, and together they build the bag. Nobody has to remember the rules, because the tables do.

title: What this really was
body: The real version ran in Excel, driven by a roughly 32 KB VBA engine. It had a small custom language for those conditional follow-up questions, a stack of decision tables with wildcards and right-side overrides (exactly what you just used), and live form behaviour so non-coders could fill it in without breaking anything. It went through four versions and shipped with a built-in tutorial so the next person could take it over. The packing example here stands in for confidential product data, but the machinery is the real thing: a way to turn scattered, tribal business logic into one structure that a team, and a computer, can both read. ← Back to projects

title: Lexaday
body: Lexaday rests on a few ideas I find convincing. Learning a word's root is leverage: one root carries a whole family of words, so a single lesson stretches far. Spacing the reviews along the forgetting curve means a word resurfaces just as it starts to fade, which is when the reminder actually does something. And generative AI does the legwork, fleshing out each root with example sentences and a mnemonic image instead of a bare definition. It all comes as one small email a day.

title: A small lesson, generated fresh each morning
body: Instead of a static flashcard, Lexaday builds a whole email around one root: what it means, the real words it shows up in, a sentence for each, and an image to anchor the meaning.

title: The learning loop
body: It comes down to roots and spacing. Learn "aud" (to hear) and audible, audition and auditorium come almost for free. And rather than drilling a root every day, Lexaday widens the gap each time it returns, one run, then two, four, eight, so a review lands just as the word starts to fade. Everyone has their own progress file, so the timing fits how much they have already seen. 1

title: Pick
body: Choose one new root the user has not seen yet, while scanning past roots for scheduled review. 2

title: Write
body: Generate a short motivational hook and clear example sentences for the words built from that root. 3

title: Imagine
body: Turn the root meaning into a visual prompt, then create a mnemonic image for the email. 4

title: Send
body: Build an HTML email, attach the generated images inline, save the new state, and repeat tomorrow.

title: What the reader receives
body: The email is plain on purpose: open Gmail, read one root, look at the picture, skim a few examples, and revisit a couple of older roots. The work that produced it stays out of sight, so the daily habit costs little. A generated email pairs the root with its meaning, a visual mnemonic, example words, and a review section when older roots are due.

title: How it is packaged
body: Personal memory Each user has a lightweight JSON record for run count, first introduction, last review, and review interval. Generated content Language generation fills in quotes and examples; image generation produces mnemonic artwork for the root. Daily automation A scheduled GitHub Actions run refreshes lessons, images, and state without needing a separate app interface. Project repo: github.com/henryhyw/lexaday (https://github.com/henryhyw/lexaday) ← Back to projects

title: Dealtracker
body: I spent a semester on exchange in Australia, and the outdoor gear I'd wanted back home, Arc'teryx, Patagonia and the like, turned out to be both cheaper than in Hong Kong or the mainland and almost always discounted somewhere. The catch was timing: a sale would surface and vanish, lost in pages and pages of products. Dealtracker is what I built so I'd stop missing them. It watches the shops for me and emails me the moment something I actually want drops to a price I'd actually pay.

title: From noisy sale pages to a short inbox digest
body: The idea is simple: pick the stores, spell out exactly what I want, brand, size, gender, minimum discount, then let a scheduled job read the sale pages for me and surface only the handful of matches worth opening. 2 Australian stores 7 brands I track 29-39% minimum discount

title: The email output
body: The output is the email itself: matching products grouped by brand and sorted by discount, each with a photo, the original and sale price, the percentage off, and a link straight to the product. Arc'teryx and The North Face digest Patagonia and Icebreaker digest

title: What happens behind the email
body: It does one narrow job: check these shops on a schedule, and email me only when something I'd actually buy has gone on sale. To do that it scrapes two Australian outdoor retailers, WildEarth and Find Your Feet. Search Build store-specific sale URLs from saved filters for brands, sizes, gender, and minimum discount. Read Open the pages in a headless browser, including dynamic product grids that need time to load. Clean Normalize brand names such as Arc'teryx versus Arcteryx and detect product gender from titles. Compare Check each result against a saved history file so the email contains new deals, not repeated noise. Notify Send a compact HTML digest only when something new passes the filters.

title: The control layer
body: Dealtracker also keeps half an eye on the inbox. Emailing it a one-word reset clears the saved history, which is handy when I want to start the baseline fresh without touching the script or the data file. Specific filters Each store has its own URL pattern, so filters are encoded in the way that store actually expects. Human-friendly output The email is built to be acted on: brand sections, product cards, prices, and links. Low-maintenance memory A local history file keeps past product links and names, preventing the same sale from being announced repeatedly. Project repo: github.com/henryhyw/dealtracker (https://github.com/henryhyw/dealtracker) ← Back to projects

title: Scrollytelling Engine
body: A course left me with a data-heavy topic to present: maps, distributions, and flows that only land once you can actually see them. Static slides could not carry it. I wanted the data to animate, and the argument to unfold as you read rather than all at once, so I built it as a web page instead, a story you move through by scrolling. Partway in, the machinery turned out to be general, so I pulled it into a small engine I can point at any subject. The deck at the bottom is one thing made with it.

title: A scene is just data
body: Here is the idea, running live. Every scene below is drawn by the engine's own code, the same builders the real deck uses. You describe a figure by its type and a few props, a registry hands it to the matching builder, and it animates itself in. Step through them. Add a scene type and it is one entry in the registry; use it and it is a few lines of data. You never touch render code.

title: See it as a story
body: The pieces above are the vocabulary. Below is the deck they were built for, running in full and embedded right here. Scroll inside it: the visuals hold while the words and data move past, which is the whole point. Cramped in the frame? Open it fullscreen ↗ (https://www.henryw.me/CCGL9074/)

title: Under the hood
body: Declarative slides Each scene is one object: its background group, its nav label, and the figures it holds. Layout, typography, and reveal timing come from shared tokens. A figure registry The type on a figure is looked up in a registry of builders, covering text, line, ridge, scatter, heatmap, geo maps, Sankey, chord, and more. Background manager Looping video layers stream per section group, so each part of the story carries its own mood without any extra markup. Zero build Plain ES modules with D3, d3-sankey, and TopoJSON loaded over CDN. It runs from any static host with nothing to compile. Live demo: henryw.me/CCGL9074 (https://www.henryw.me/CCGL9074/) Project repo: github.com/henryhyw/CCGL9074 (https://github.com/henryhyw/CCGL9074) ← Back to projects

title: My Early Adventures in Computer Science
body: Here are the adventures I went through during my junior high and high school years. I've highlighted some main projects from each year. I'll admit upfront: a lot of what I did back then was pretty naive. But who hasn't had a naive phase, right? Honestly, I might still be a bit naive now, and that's okay.

title: How It All Started
body: I fell in love with computer science when I was 13, in my first year of junior high school. My computer science teacher demonstrated how a batch script can create folders automatically. From that moment, I was fascinated by how computers could be programmed to do all sorts of cool things. I taught myself the basics of Java and Python and started building simple apps, like a contact notebook app in Java and an advanced number guessing game with user accounts and game history logged in text files. Here are some screenshots of this first app (contact notebook) I made.

title: Batch Downloading Scientific American Episodes
body: In my first year of junior high, I decided to practice English and discovered the podcast called Scientific American. At that time, I was using an MP3 player that supported playing audio, images, and texts. So, I developed an app to batch download audios, scripts, and images from Scientific American episodes. The app included a user account system that could record the episodes a user had downloaded and continue from where they left off next time. Here are some screenshots of this app.

title: Chrome Extension Experiments
body: In my second year of junior high, I started exploring Chrome extensions. Living in Mainland China made me curious about things that weren't easily accessible, so I'd use a VPN to access Google-related stuff. It amazed me that these small apps in the browser could do so much, like turning the screen dark for YouTube videos. So, I learned how to develop and publish Chrome extensions and created a few: Easy QR Code : Displays QR codes for the current website, makes your own QR codes, and converts text to QR codes. Page Muter : Mutes the audio or video of a specific tab. Image Fetcher : Batch downloads images from the current website. SA Fetcher : Batch downloads audios, scripts, and images from Scientific American episodes. This is a simplified version of the previous SA Fetcher app. WeChat Spamming : Automatically sends various kinds of spam messages on WeChat (I made this to prank my friends, and surprisingly, it even has 167 users according to the developer dashboard). Here are some screenshots of the developer dashboard and some of the extensions.

title: Music Streaming Hacks
body: In my first year of high school, my main interest was building an app for downloading paid songs from music streaming services in China. I now realize that this was illegal, but at the time, I wasn't aware of that. Although I'm not proud of it, I still want to mention it because it was a masterpiece for a first-year high school student. I did a lot of research on web scraping and managed to crack the links of paid songs from various music streaming services. With this app, users could download songs with the link of the song page from QQ Music, Netease Music, KuGou Music, and KuWo Music, with the desired sound quality. Users could also batch download songs from a singer with a specified number of pages. I even created a user account system where some functions, like downloading lossless audio, were only available to VIP users. It took me several months to develop this app, but it only lasted for half a year because I later realized it wasn't quite legal, so I shut it down. Despite that, it remains one of my fondest memories. By that time, it had 413 users, with 228 QQ Music playlist downloads, 1294 QQ Music single downloads, 523 NetEase Music single downloads, 23 KuGou Music single downloads, and 47 KuWo Music single downloads. Here are some screenshots of the app, which is in Simplified Chinese since the users were from Mainland China.

title: Building Kiwiview's Website
body: In my second year of high school, I started building a website for Kiwiview International Limited, a New Zealand company co-owned by my parents and a Kiwi. Since the business was just starting, they let me try building the website. I taught myself HTML, JavaScript, CSS, and SQL. It took me months to deploy it on an IIS server on Tencent Cloud. The deployment process was really complicated for a beginner, and I spent a lot of time researching it.

title: Simple Automation
body: In my third year of high school, I came across a mental arithmetic and "24-point" game event. It was an online game event held at our school. I created a script to automatically solve the problems, achieving really high scores. The script recognized the content on the screen, used algorithms to solve the problems, and then instructed the computer to input the answers. Everything was done automatically and repeatedly until time was up. Here are some videos of the script I recorded. Your browser does not support the video tag. Your browser does not support the video tag.

title: Econographer, an Economics Graphs Generator
body: In my third year of high school, I started to wonder if the graphs we needed for our economics class could be generated by software. After searching online and not finding anything that met my expectations, I decided to create my own solution. I developed an Office Word add-in called "Econographer," specifically designed for generating the various types of economics graphs used in AP and IB courses. These graphs are highly customizable to fit any requirement. Here's a full demonstration video, recorded by me, showing how it works. Your browser does not support the video tag. I hope you enjoyed reading about my early journey with computer science! ← Back to projects

title: Fragments
body: Loose fragments of thought and life. Fragment entries are indexed from the shared fragment data source.

title: Rebuilding Another State Inside Me
body: Empathy may be less like directly receiving another feeling, and more like rebuilding another state inside my own system from the signals it leaves outside.

title: Rebuilding Another State Inside Me (2)
body:
I cannot actually enter another person’s state.

This sounds obvious, but I think it matters more than it first seems. Another person’s real state is inside them. Their body, memory, history, habits, fear, shame, desire, and the way a certain event touches all of these things are not directly available to me.

What I can see is what comes out.

Words. Facial expression. Tone. Silence. Movement. A change in posture. A story they tell. Something they avoid saying. The situation around them. These are outputs, or traces, from a system I cannot directly open.

So empathy cannot be simple access.

It is more like reconstruction.

Another person gives off signals, and my own system tries to build a state that corresponds to them. I take in the visible part, then use my own memory, body, experience, imagination, and learned sense of people to generate something inside me.

That thing inside me is not exactly their feeling.

But it is also not just my ordinary feeling.

It is a state produced in me, but pulled by them.

This is where empathy starts to become interesting to me. It is not only about whether I know what someone feels. That is too shallow. The real question is what kind of state gets generated inside me after I receive their signals.

At a shallow level, I may only label the state.

He is sad. She is embarrassed. This person is anxious. This person feels left out.

That kind of recognition is useful, but it still feels outside. It is like I have classified the output, but nothing much has happened inside my own system.

At a deeper level, the other person’s state starts changing my state.

My attention moves toward them. My body may tighten a little. My voice may become softer. I may become more careful with my words. I may feel an urge to stop explaining, to comfort, to stay quiet, to protect, or to move closer.

This is probably why I keep thinking empathy needs some kind of internal signal.

Not necessarily the same signal as the other person has. That would

title: Rebuilding Another State Inside Me (3)
body:
This is why I do not think empathy is automatically pure or correct. A person can empathize deeply and still be wrong. Sometimes the deeper reconstruction may even become more dangerous, because I start trusting the feeling inside me too much. I feel something strongly, so I think I have reached them. But maybe I have reached a version of them that my own system produced.

This does not make empathy fake.

It only means empathy is not a direct copy of another mind.

It is a model, but not a cold one. It is a reconstruction, but not only cognitive. It is a state inside me that tries to correspond to a state outside me.

Then self-empathy becomes a special case.

At first it sounds easier. If the state I am trying to feel belongs to myself, then maybe I do not need to reconstruct another system. It is my own system. My own body history. My own memory line. My own past.

But I do not think it is that simple.

The past version of me is also not directly available to the present me. A hidden part of me is also not fully open. A feeling I pushed away, a state I did not understand at the time, a pressure I lived through but later compressed into a story, all of these are not just sitting there in their original form.

They leave traces.

A memory. A reaction. A kind of avoidance. A bodily discomfort. A preference I cannot explain. A sudden resistance to something. A feeling that a certain old moment still matters, even though I have already explained it many times.

So self-empathy may also be reconstruction.

The present me takes traces from a past or hidden me, and tries to rebuild that state inside the current system.

This is not the same as self-understanding.

I can explain myself very well and still not really be close to myself.

I can say why I reacted that way. I can say where the pattern came from. I can say what kind of experience shaped it. I can turn it into a clean story.

But sometimes that explanation feels too far above the state.

It is like I have descr

title: Rebuilding Another State Inside Me (4)
body:
But this proxy is different from language. It does not explain the state as quickly. It lets the state sit there in a form. I can look at it before I know exactly what it means.

That looking matters.

The image came from me, but once it is outside, it is not fully trapped inside me anymore. I can face it. I can move around it. I can notice what part pulls my attention. I can feel whether it makes me tense, calm, sad, embarrassed, or unexpectedly familiar.

In that moment, the image may help rebuild a state that language could only summarize.

Maybe this is why something like mandala can feel connected to self-empathy. Not because drawing a shape magically heals anything, but because it gives a hard-to-access internal state an external surface. The state becomes something I can meet again.

So empathy, self-empathy, language, and drawing may all be different ways of dealing with states that are not directly accessible.

With another person, I see outputs and rebuild a state inside me.

With a past or hidden self, I see traces and rebuild a state inside the present me.

With language, I compress the state into words.

With drawing, I may let the state become visible before it is fully explained.

None of these gives perfect access.

But maybe perfect access is not the point.

The point is whether a state that was outside my immediate reach can become active enough inside me to change how I feel, how I understand, or how I act.

That state may belong to someone else. It may belong to a past version of me. It may belong to a part of me that has not yet become clear.

Either way, I am not simply receiving it.

I am rebuilding it inside me, using the only system I have.

title: The Visible Part of the Mind
body: Consciousness may not be the whole mind becoming visible, but the part of the mind that becomes reachable through the lens of the current moment.

title: The Visible Part of the Mind (2)
body:
Sometimes I feel that what I consciously think is much smaller than what is actually moving inside my mind.

A thought appears, and because it appears clearly enough, I may feel that this is the thought itself. I can hold it, say it, write it down, or continue from it. But very often, the thing that appears in consciousness is probably only a small visible part of something much larger.

Before I know what I think, many things may already be moving. Memory, mood, body, recent experience, language, old associations, unfinished thoughts, attention, and the current situation may all be involved. Most of them do not appear directly. I do not see all of them as separate causes. I only receive the result.

That result may be a sentence. It may be a feeling. It may be a sudden preference. It may be a vague sense that something is wrong. It may be the feeling that I understand something, even before I can explain why.

So maybe consciousness is not where a thought begins.

Maybe consciousness is where something becomes reachable.

This distinction matters to me. Something can already be shaping me before I can consciously access it. A memory can affect my reaction without appearing as a memory. A mood can change how I read a situation without announcing itself as mood. A past experience can make one idea feel natural and another idea feel wrong, even when I cannot immediately explain the path.

When something finally becomes conscious, it is not necessarily being created at that moment. It may simply have become available to the part of me that can notice, hold, name, report, and continue working on it.

But even then, what becomes conscious is still not the whole structure.

It is more like a projection.

I do not mean projection in a precise mathematical sense. I just mean that a larger internal state is being shown through a smaller surface. Something complex becomes visible as something simpler. A whole background of memory, emotion, body, and attention becomes one thou

title: The Visible Part of the Mind (3)
body:
This may be why the same thought can change when I return to it later. I may think I am continuing the same idea, but actually I am reading it through a different state. The old material is still there, but the current lens has changed. New mood, new language, new context, new pressure, new experience. So the thought comes out differently.

This also makes me think that language is not the first compression.

I used to think about expression as the moment when a vague thought becomes compressed into language. That still feels right. But maybe before language compresses thought, consciousness has already compressed it once.

A large hidden state becomes a conscious surface. Then the conscious surface becomes a sentence. Then the sentence becomes a written note. Then the written note becomes something I later read and continue from.

At each step, something is preserved and something is lost.

This is why I should not fully trust the conscious version either. It is closer to me than the written version, but it is still not the whole thing. It is already shaped by attention, access, mood, language, and the current way I am reading myself.

Still, I need it.

Without the visible part, I cannot work with anything. I cannot hold the whole hidden structure directly. I need something to appear. I need a sentence, a feeling, a rough image, a small handle. Consciousness gives me that handle.

But the handle is not the whole mind.

Maybe that is the strange thing about consciousness. It feels like the place where I meet myself most directly, but it may also be one of the first places where I have already been simplified.

What I can report is not nothing. But it is not the whole thing.

It is the visible part of the mind, made reachable through the lens of the current moment.

title: Before Defining Understanding
body: Maybe understanding should not be defined too quickly, because the word often gathers many different questions before we have separated them.

title: Before Defining Understanding (2)
body:
I do not want to define understanding too early.

Not because the word is meaningless. It clearly points to something important. But it also gathers too many things before I have separated them.

Sometimes understanding means being able to give the right answer. Sometimes it means being able to explain. Sometimes it means transfer, generalization, correction, compression, use, experience, grounding, or consciousness. These things are related, but they are not the same.

So if I define understanding too quickly, I may only be choosing one layer and pretending I have answered the whole question.

This matters for both people and machines. A student may solve a problem correctly without really knowing why. A person may explain something fluently while missing the structure underneath. A model may give a good answer without failing or adapting in the same way a person would. In all of these cases, the word understanding feels tempting, but also unstable.

That is why I think the first step should not be to decide whether something understands.

The first step should be to ask which part of understanding I am talking about.

Am I asking whether it can answer? Whether it can explain? Whether it can use the idea in a new situation? Whether it can notice when the surface changes but the structure remains? Whether it can revise itself after being wrong? Whether it has experience behind the behavior?

These questions may all belong near understanding, but they should not be collapsed into one word too quickly.

The same problem appears in the usual contrast between correlation and causation. People often say that machines learn correlations while humans understand causes. I think there is something right in that, but it is also too clean.

Humans also begin with regularities. We see one thing often appear with another, and later we expect them together. That alone is not yet causation.

But a regularity can become an assumption. The assumption can guide action. Then the world

title: Expression, Recording, and the Compressed Version of Thought
body: Expression preserves a thought by turning it into language, but the same act also compresses and reshapes it.

title: Expression, Recording, and the Compressed Version of Thought (2)
body:
When a thought is still in my head, it is usually not a sentence.

It is more like a mass of things. There is a feeling, a direction, some past experience, and connections I cannot quite explain even to myself. Most of the time, I do not first have a complete sentence and then simply say it out loud. It is more like I have something vague but real, and language cuts it apart, puts it in order, and compresses it into a form other people can understand.

That matters. Without language, many thoughts would just stay inside my head. Once I say something, it becomes something I can work with. Other people can understand it. They can push back on it. I can look at it again. It can be saved, remembered, organized further, and put into the next round of thinking.

So expression is not a bad thing. Many thoughts only become usable after they are expressed. Before that, they are just a state in the mind. After being said, they have a clearer shape. That shape lets the thought be transmitted, discussed, changed, and picked up again later.

But expression is not free.

Language asks you to choose words, choose an order, and choose boundaries. Many things that originally existed together have to be said one after another. Some feelings are fine, mixed, and hard to separate, but in order to express them, they have to be cut into parts. After being cut, the thought is indeed clearer and easier to transmit, but it is no longer the original thing.

It has become a discrete version.

That discrete version is useful. Without it, thought cannot be taken out. But it also necessarily loses something. It keeps some main structure and turns the thought into a form that can be transmitted, saved, organized, and operated on. But the parts that did not enter language are temporarily compressed away.

This is why I think compression is central here. Expression and recording do not simply move a thought out of the mind. They first compress the thought into a version that can be processed. That

title: Expression, Recording, and the Compressed Version of Thought (3)
body:
Different languages may work this way too. Not in the simple sense that one language makes people more rational or anything like that, but in a more specific sense: different languages may cut experience differently. A continuous thought may naturally become certain blocks in one language, and different blocks in another language. If the cut changes, the later path of thought may also change.

That may be why saying something in another language sometimes feels like it is no longer quite the same thing. It is not only a question of whether the translation is accurate. The original mass has been cut again.

This also makes me think about where artificial intelligence fits.

AI is very good at working with things that have already been expressed. If you give it a paragraph, a pile of notes, a few claims, or scattered materials, it can summarize, organize, classify, recombine, and find connections. It can make already-discretized material clearer, and it can point out arrangements I may not have noticed yet.

But what it receives is still the version I have already said.

The original thought does not begin as text. It comes from experience, memory, emotion, details of life, bodily state, unclear intuitions, and many connections that have not been fully expressed. By the time I tell these things to AI, I have already compressed them once. It is processing the material after that compression, not the mass that existed before language cut it apart.

So AI is useful to me, but its place is closer to an organizer and a reflecting surface than to the source of the thought. It can help me straighten out what has already been written, show me where things repeat, where they connect, and where they remain unclear. But I should not mistake that organization for thought itself.

What I need to watch out for is continuing to work only on the version AI has organized, while forgetting to bring back new experience, new hesitation, and new intuition. The thought may become smoother

title: Expression, Recording, and the Compressed Version of Thought (4)
body:
This is not only a problem of being too early. Being too early does create problems. If a thought is organized into a concept right after it appears, it can easily become narrower. But even if it is not too early, even if I have thought about it for a long time, once it is written down, it still forms a version. That version will later influence me.

I may continue thinking along its path. I may also be led by it.

This is what I need to be careful about.

These notes matter because they mean I do not have to start from zero every time. They are like handles that let me hold onto things that would otherwise be scattered. But the handle is not the thing itself. If I trust the written versions too much, they will turn from handles into boundaries.

I may begin to feel: this thought is exactly this. But actually, it was only written this way at that moment.

So I do not want to treat these notes as definitions. They are more like slices from a certain day. They record where I had thought up to at that time, but they should not decide where I can go later. Later, I can completely change my mind, rewrite them, discover that they connect elsewhere, or realize that they were not that important after all.

Titles are useful. Labels are useful. A little classification is useful. Without any of them, things become messy and hard to find. But they should mainly be for retrieval, not for making boundaries. A thought that today seems to be about language may tomorrow connect to memory, and the day after that to consciousness. It should not be stuck forever in one place just because it was first placed there.

Similarly, the fact that a thought has not been developed right now does not mean it has been excluded. It just has not grown there yet. Maybe it will connect back later, maybe it will not. Placing something too early on the irrelevant side is also a form of over-organization.

I prefer to see these thoughts as a graph that slowly grows. Each note is a node, with some lines

title: Labels, Goals, and Rules as Ways of Shaping People
body: Labels, goals, and rules make a complex person readable, expected, and constrained, then shape that person in return.

title: Labels, Goals, and Rules as Ways of Shaping People (2)
body:
If thoughts can be compressed by language, then people can be compressed by labels.

A person is also a very complicated mass of things. There are experiences, behaviors, habits, desires, fears, interests, abilities, and tendencies even the person may not be able to explain clearly. Many of these things are continuous. They do not naturally come in neat blocks. A person is not born already divided into personality, occupation, research direction, hobbies, values, and life stages. Very often, these divisions are cut out later so that the person can be understood and communicated.

But we do need those cuts.

Other people cannot directly read a person’s full internal structure. What you have done, what you have thought, why you react in certain ways, why you care about some things and not others, all of this is too complicated. You cannot hand over your complete self every time. Most of the time, you can only hand over a compressed version.

That is why labels are useful.

Personality types are one kind. A professional identity is one kind. A research direction is one kind. A personal profile is one kind. The way other people describe what kind of person you are is also one kind. We say we belong to a certain type, fit a certain direction, care about certain questions, or resemble a certain group of people. This makes us easier for others to read, and also easier for ourselves to organize.

A label is a bit like a language for describing people. It takes a continuous, complex, hard-to-transfer person and compresses that person into a format that can travel more easily. Through it, other people can quickly get a rough sense of who you are, what you care about, who you may be similar to, and how you differ from others. You can also use it to gather scattered experiences, interests, behaviors, and thoughts into one place.

This is not bad in itself. Without labels, people are hard to understand and hard to remember. Without some kind of identity narrative, many experienc

title: Labels, Goals, and Rules as Ways of Shaping People (3)
body:
More than that, a label can sometimes work like a training signal from the outside world.

The outside world cannot see a person’s complete internal structure. It can only make compressed judgments from behavior: this person is smart, this person is outgoing, this person is suited for research, this person has leadership, this person is unreliable, this person belongs to a certain type. At first, these judgments may only be summaries based on some observed behavior. But once they appear, they begin to change the environment.

People treat you through those labels. They give you different expectations, opportunities, feedback, and limitations. If you are seen as suited for something, you may receive more opportunities related to it, and you may also be expected to keep performing in that direction. If you are seen as unsuited for something, you may be given fewer chances to try, and eventually you may stop trying yourself.

So labels from the outside are not only descriptions. They also change a person’s training environment.

They change how other people respond to you, and they also change how you predict yourself. You begin to know what others expect you to become, and you begin to adjust your behavior. Sometimes this adjustment is only on the surface. But over time, the internal structure may also change. Habits change. Abilities change. Desires change. Even your understanding of yourself can change.

A loop forms here.

A person’s internal structure produces behavior. Behavior is compressed by the outside world into labels. Labels become expectations, opportunities, rewards, and limits. These forms of feedback return to the person and continue shaping later behavior and internal structure.

So a label is not a static sticker. It is part of a feedback system.

This process is not necessarily bad. A good label, or an accurate expectation, may push someone in a better direction. If someone says you are suited for something, you may become more willing to invest in

title: Labels, Goals, and Rules as Ways of Shaping People (4)
body:
Sometimes a person’s internal structure has not yet naturally produced the output that society expects. In other words, their impulses, habits, desires, attention, or existing tendencies are not yet aligned with a certain social requirement. In that case, society does not wait for the internal structure to slowly change. It first uses rules to constrain behavior.

Law, moral norms, politeness, institutions, school requirements, and family expectations are all rules of this kind. They are also a form of discretization. They compress complex situations into explicit requirements: do not do this, say this instead, behave this way in this situation, suppress this impulse, this behavior will be punished.

Rules are not quite the same as labels. Labels often say “what you are” or “what others expect you to become.” Goals say “what you want to become.” Rules are more like “what you at least must not do” or “what output you must produce.”

Very often, rules are not natural reactions at first.

When a person sees something, they may first understand it through their existing internal structure. Past experience, habits, prejudice, desire, and fear may first shape what they notice, what they think of, and how they want to respond. This first reaction does not come from nowhere. It comes from internal representations that have already formed.

But immediately after that, another rule may intervene. That rule is also in the mind, but it has not been fully internalized. It may come from law, morality, politeness, education, or social expectation. It tells you: do not say this, do not do this, do not keep your attention there, do not let the first reaction become the final behavior.

So the behavior that finally appears may not be the behavior naturally generated by the internal structure. It may be the result after rule-based override.

The outside world sees compliant behavior, but the internal path may be completely different. One person may simply not have that impulse. Anothe

title: Labels, Goals, and Rules as Ways of Shaping People (5)
body:
External discrete rules slowly become part of the internal structure. An originally strong tendency may be worn down, weakened, or at least made easier to suppress. Whether a rule can be internalized, and how deeply it can be internalized, is itself a question. Not every rule truly changes internal structure. Some remain surface compliance. Some change habits. Some enter attention and judgment more deeply.

And social rules are not always right. A rule is an external constraint mechanism, not truth itself. It can protect people, but it can also limit people. It can help internal structure become more stable, but it can also press a person into an unreasonable shape.

So I do not want to simply say labels are bad, goals are bad, or rules are bad.

They are clearly useful. Without labels, others have a hard time reading you, and you have a hard time organizing yourself. Without goals, many behaviors may not hold together. Without rules, society cannot rely only on everyone’s naturally formed internal structure to maintain order.

The question is not whether we should have these things. The question is whether we remember that none of them is neutral.

Labels make people readable. Goals give people a point to move toward. Rules override output when the internal structure has not yet naturally aligned.

All of them compress complex people into forms that are easier to handle: readable, expected, trainable, and constrained. Then they shape people in return through feedback, opportunities, rewards, punishments, repetition, and internalization.

AI can also be understood through this problem, but here the issue is not whether it is the source of thought. The issue is that it can become a training signal.

It does not only help me organize content. It also presents a way of moving thought forward: turn something vague into several points, turn those points into a structure, turn the structure into a next step, and turn the next step into an output that looks complete. This

title: Labels, Goals, and Rules as Ways of Shaping People (6)
body:
Being misunderstood is bad, of course. But it is even stranger when people seem to understand you, while in fact they only understand a wrongly compressed version. The clearer and more complete that version looks, the easier it is for people to believe that it is you.

That is stranger than simply being forgotten. Being forgotten is disappearance. Being wrongly summarized leaves behind something that looks like you, but whose inner structure may not be yours.

So what interests me about labels, goals, and rules is not only whether they are accurate or right. It is their two-way effect.

They come from the person, or from society’s demands on the person. Then they return to the person.

They first make the person readable, expected, and constrained. Then they begin to change how the person continues becoming themselves.

Labels can help us understand a person, and they can also help a person organize themselves. Goals can give continuous behavior a direction. Rules can first override outputs that have not yet naturally aligned.

But once they appear, they are no longer merely explanations or restrictions. They become expectation, feedback, training, constraint, and force.

They compress a complex person into a version that can be handled by society and by the person themselves. And then they shape the person who has been compressed.

title: Memory Compresses Time, but the Self Is Changed by Passing Through It
body: Memory turns continuous life into stages and stories, but the self is shaped by actually moving through time.

title: Memory Compresses Time, but the Self Is Changed by Passing Through It (2)
body:
Life, while it is happening, does not really come in sections.

It does not tell you: this part is high school, this part is university, this year is a turning point, this city means growth. Real time feels more like something that keeps flowing forward. Many changes do not have clear borders while they are happening. You simply live one day after another, slowly change, and only when you look back do you realize that you seem to have entered another stage.

But memory does not seem very good at preserving that continuity.

The closer something is, the more detail it has. Today still has temperature. Something said yesterday still has a tone. A recent experience has not yet fully become a story. But once time moves further away, many things begin to be compressed. A year becomes a few keywords. A place becomes a feeling. A relationship becomes a few images. Many things that were once fine-grained eventually become only “that period.”

And this compression does not happen evenly. It has a temporal gradient. The closer something is to the present, the lighter the compression, and the details remain relatively loose. The further away it is, the heavier the compression, and many things are folded into stages, labels, stories, and a few representative scenes. Distant life is not really made only of a few keywords. It is just stored by memory at a higher compression rate.

So sometimes I feel that so-called life stages may not be the structure of life itself, but a structure that memory later adds to life.

This is not necessarily bad. If the past remained there in full detail, maybe people could not keep moving forward. A stage is like a folder. It gathers too many scattered things into one place. You do not need to reopen every detail each time. You only need to say “that time,” and many things come back with it.

But compression always loses something.

You may remember a certain year as a painful year, but inside that year there were probably ordinary afternoons, funn

title: Memory Compresses Time, but the Self Is Changed by Passing Through It (3)
body:
I do not really like saying that the future self is another person. That saying has something to it. The future version of me may think differently, like different things, care about different things. He may even fail to understand why the present version of me cares so much about certain problems. But if I simply call the future self another person, something feels wrong. It is as if the continuous line in between has been cut.

A person is not a fixed point. A person is more like a trajectory that keeps changing.

The version of me at each moment is not exactly the same. What I see today changes tomorrow’s judgment. What happens tomorrow changes how I understand today the day after. Memory changes. The body changes. Relationships change. The internal representations in my mind also change. It is hard to say which slice is the real me.

But these slices are not connected randomly. They change into one another little by little.

The future me is not a stranger who suddenly appears in the distance. He is the state of this current system after many updates. Each step may be tiny, so tiny that it is hard to notice at the time. But many small changes together can become a clearly different person.

That is why the path in between matters.

If you only look at two time points, a person can certainly seem very different. The me from ten years ago and the me now may look like two versions. But what makes both of them still me may not be how similar the two versions are. It may be the continuous path between them. Even if another person knows my experiences and even many of my memories, he cannot directly become me, because he did not move through the path from the inside.

Maybe each momentary version of me is only a slice of a longer structure. If you look at the slices separately, they may be quite different. But once you put the time dimension back, they are not isolated people. They are the same thing taking different shapes at different positions.

So the self is not

title: Memory Compresses Time, but the Self Is Changed by Passing Through It (4)
body:
What makes it even stranger is that the person after the jump may not feel that anything is missing. He may feel that he really did go through it. Then the question becomes harder: if even the subjective feeling of continuity is preserved, what is still irreplaceable about actually experiencing it?

Should he take the pill?

My first instinct is yes. It is simple: if the final state is the same, and the memory is the same, why actually go through the pain? Pain itself is not something worth collecting. If I can avoid the pain and keep the result, that sounds reasonable.

But many people would not choose it. They would feel that the painful period is also part of life. Even if you remember it afterward, if you did not actually live through it, something still seems missing.

I have always found this strange. If the memory is truly complete, and the person after the jump believes he has lived through it, has the same lessons, the same memories, and the same result, then what exactly does “actually living through it” add?

Maybe memory and experience are still not the same thing.

Memory is like the result, but experience is the process. Pain does not only leave a conclusion at the end. It changes a person second by second in the middle. Many things are not written into memory all at once. They slowly change you while you are enduring them. You may think that receiving the same memory at the end is enough, but perhaps what really changes you is each morning you wake up, each moment of loss, each time you think you are fine and then fall back again.

This brings me back to the earlier question: is the self made of results and memories, or is it made of the process of actually passing through time?

If the self is a trajectory in time, then skipping pain is not only skipping an unpleasant experience. It may also skip a stretch of continuous change. The person after the jump has memory, and even the feeling of having gone through it, but he did not actually pass through t

title: Memory Compresses Time, but the Self Is Changed by Passing Through It (5)
body:
I just understand better now why some people would not.

The self is not a photograph. It is also not just a compressed memoir.

It is more like a movement.

No moment is the final version. But each moment grows out of the one before it.

Memory compresses time into stages, making the past something that can be preserved, narrated, and understood again. But what truly changes a person may not only be those stages and stories. It may also be the continuous passage through time that was never fully preserved.

What we remember later are chapters. What we lived then was a whole line.

title: Satisfaction Is Not a Fixed Reward
body: Satisfaction is better understood as a changing signal generated through behavior, time, individual difference, and interpretation.

title: Satisfaction Is Not a Fixed Reward (2)
body:
I sometimes wonder whether happiness and satisfaction can be treated as the same thing.

This is obviously debatable. Some people would say happiness is not just satisfaction. It also includes meaning, long-term goals, relationships, a sense of value, and even growth through pain. But I tend to first understand happiness as a kind of satisfaction. The important point is that satisfaction cannot be defined too narrowly. It cannot just mean whether something feels good right now.

If satisfaction is understood more broadly, it can include many things.

Eating something good in the moment is a kind of satisfaction. Delayed gratification is also a kind of satisfaction. Finishing a goal is a kind of satisfaction. Controlling an impulse is a kind of satisfaction. Helping someone may also be a kind of satisfaction. Getting a good deal may be a kind of satisfaction. Even feeling that I made the more rational choice can itself bring satisfaction.

So delayed gratification is not necessarily the opposite of satisfaction. It may simply be another form of satisfaction.

Some people treat immediate gratification as lower and delayed gratification as higher. I think that is too simple. A person who chooses delayed gratification may not be rejecting satisfaction. They may be getting satisfaction from the delay itself, from future payoff, from self-control, or from the feeling that they made the better choice.

In other words, they are not avoiding satisfaction. They are choosing another satisfaction curve.

What is called more rational may not be separate from satisfaction. It may be that, in that person’s system, the choice is expected to produce a stronger, more stable, or longer-lasting satisfaction signal. Rationality and satisfaction do not have to be opposites. Sometimes rationality itself is a source of satisfaction.

I do not need to reduce satisfaction to one specific biological measure, but I can at least think of it as an internal feedback signal. Different activities t

title: Satisfaction Is Not a Fixed Reward (3)
body:
For some people, getting a discount is not just about saving money. It feels like a small win. There is the search, the comparison, the surprise of discovering a good deal, the feeling of being good at living, and the sense that resources are under better control. It can even feel like a little game: I found it, I judged correctly, I won a little.

If the process itself gives that person satisfaction, then it is not simply a waste of time.

For another person, the same process may produce no joy at all. It may only feel like trouble, exhaustion, anxiety, and a waste of attention. For that person, buying at the original price may be more rational, because what they save is not a little money, but time, energy, and mental space.

So the same behavior may be rational in different ways for different people.

It is not because rationality is arbitrary. It is because the behavior produces different satisfaction signals once it enters different internal systems. From the outside, both people are spending time looking for discounts, but internally the returns are different, so the rational calculation is different too. You cannot judge whether something is worth it only by asking how much time was spent and how much money was saved. You also have to ask what the person received from the process.

Opportunity cost should not be calculated only in money and time. More accurately, opportunity cost is the satisfaction you give up from another possible activity.

If someone spends an hour looking for discounts and enjoys the process, then that hour is not only a cost. It is also part of the return. But if someone does not enjoy the process at all, then that hour is closer to a pure cost.

This makes me think that satisfaction is not something the outside world simply hands to a person. It is not that one behavior is naturally joyful and another is naturally boring. Often, the behavior itself is only the material. The actual satisfaction is generated through a person’s internal s

title: Satisfaction Is Not a Fixed Reward (4)
body:
By creation, I do not mean forcing oneself to be happy or rationalizing everything bad. Creating satisfaction is not pretending that a bad thing is good. It is redesigning the relationship between a behavior and oneself so that the behavior can genuinely produce some positive signal.

People can use attention, interpretation, habit design, and goal setting to make some originally neutral activities capable of producing satisfaction.

Take housework. For some people, it is just a burden. But if someone understands it as restoring order, organizing space, and making life feel controllable again, it may begin to produce some satisfaction. Exercise may start as pain, but once it connects to progress, rhythm, and bodily control, it becomes another kind of signal. Volunteering may look from the outside like giving up time, but for some people, it is not a loss. It produces value and connection.

This does not mean everyone should think this way. It also does not mean everything deserves to be repackaged. Some things are simply draining. Some pain is simply pain. We should not turn every negative thing into meaning just to manufacture satisfaction.

But I do think a person can ask: are there things that I currently find joyless, but that could produce some positive signal if I understood, organized, and experienced them differently?

This may matter more than simply chasing stronger stimulation.

Strong stimulation easily saturates the system. If something arrives too quickly, too strongly, and too easily, it can certainly create immediate satisfaction, but it can also change the scale of satisfaction. After that, the same thing is no longer enough. More, faster, and stronger is required. The old high point becomes the new baseline, and ordinary life begins to feel flat.

So satisfaction is not only about quantity. It is also about how satisfaction is generated.

Some satisfaction comes from the result. Some satisfaction comes from approaching the result. Some satisfaction

title: Satisfaction Is Not a Fixed Reward (5)
body:
The satisfaction system adapts, compares, recalibrates, and generates different signals under different interpretations.

So a person with better living conditions is not always more satisfied. A person with fewer resources is not completely unable to generate satisfaction. The former may become saturated because things arrive too quickly. The latter may still find structure, expectation, and process in small things.

Of course, this should not be used to cover up real problems. Poverty, illness, oppression, and long-term stress cannot be solved by simply “changing perspective.” External conditions still matter. But once basic conditions are considered, when we talk about quality of life, the way satisfaction is generated also matters.

I increasingly feel that satisfaction is not a fixed thing, and not a simple external reward.

It is more like an internal signal that a person keeps generating through time, behavior, and interpretation.

We are affected by external stimulation. We are also affected by how we interpret things. We are affected by immediate outcomes. We are also affected by waiting and process. We are affected by how others evaluate us. We are also affected by how we understand ourselves.

So if happiness can be understood as satisfaction, satisfaction should not be understood as a simple number.

It is more like a changing system. It can saturate, shift, be trained, and be reinterpreted. It can be given by the outside world, and it can also be slowly cultivated by the person themselves.

Maybe one important part of life is not only finding more things that satisfy us, but learning how to let more ordinary things produce a small signal that makes life worth continuing.

title: Oysters and Black Truffle Toast at Queen Victoria Market
body: A Melbourne food memory from my last day before leaving exchange: oysters with lemon juice and black truffle toast at Queen Victoria Market.

title: Oysters and Black Truffle Toast at Queen Victoria Market (2)
body:
Queen Victoria Market in Melbourne gave me one of those food memories I will keep thinking about after leaving the city.

The oysters were fresh, cold, and clean, and they were really good with lemon juice. That sharpness made the oyster taste even clearer. The black truffle toast was also unexpectedly satisfying: warm, rich, fragrant, and direct.

What makes it especially memorable is that I went there with my sister on my last day before leaving Melbourne exchange. It was not just about the food, but also about the timing: one last market morning, one last meal before leaving, and something simple that became tied to the whole exchange period.

This is one of the foods I will keep missing after leaving Melbourne.

title: Chaoshan Beef Hot Pot in Guangzhou
body: A Guangzhou food memory with my father: Chaoshan beef hot pot, especially beef balls and diaolong, eaten without sauce for the clean beef flavor.

title: Chaoshan Beef Hot Pot in Guangzhou (2)
body:
When I travelled to Guangzhou with my father, we went to eat Chaoshan beef hot pot together. That made the meal memorable to me, not only because the food was good, but because it became part of that trip with him.

I really like Chaoshan-style beef. I especially like the beef balls and diaolong. The beef balls have that springy texture and deep beef taste, and diaolong has a clean, tender freshness that works very well in hot pot.

I also tend not to dip it in sauce. The plain beef flavor is already good enough. I like the original, fresh taste of the beef itself, without covering it up too much.

title: AI-Generated Kitten Videos and Manufactured Cuteness
body: AI-generated kitten videos are a good example of synthetic content still reaching human senses and triggering real emotional responses.

title: AI-Generated Kitten Videos and Manufactured Cuteness (2)
body:
I have seen many AI-generated videos on social media where kittens interact with humans, and also some more surreal ones, like a plush watermelon being opened and turning out to contain little kittens. I find them very cute, and clearly many other people like them too.

What interests me is not only that the videos are cute. They are a good example of generative AI content triggering human emotion. The kittens are not real in the usual sense, but the content still reaches human perception: softness, smallness, eye contact, movement, care, surprise. These signals can still produce emotional fluctuation.

That makes this kind of AI-generated content valuable. It can create things that touch human senses and emotions, even when the objects are synthetic. But it also needs caution, because the same ability can be abused. If generated content can reliably press emotional buttons, then it can also be used to manipulate attention, sympathy, desire, or belief.

So I do not think the value of generative AI content is only whether it is realistic. Sometimes its value is that it can generate emotionally meaningful sensory patterns. But precisely because those patterns work on people, they need to be treated carefully.

title: A Conversation About Identity
body: After taking part in an interview about growing up between Shanghai and Taiwanese family roots, I found a clearer way to describe identity: not confused, but layered.

title: A Conversation About Identity (2)
body:
I once took part in an interview about identity and growing up with a Taiwanese family background while living mostly in Shanghai. It was not something I had planned to write publicly at first, but the conversation helped me organize many scattered feelings that had existed in the background for a long time.

What stayed with me most was not a dramatic conclusion, but a quieter one: identity is not always a single answer. It can be made of several layers: where I grew up, where my family comes from, what languages and habits shaped me, what places feel familiar, and how I introduce myself in different contexts.

The interview helped me see that this is not necessarily confusion. It is more like a layered structure. Different parts become more visible in different situations. Sometimes Shanghai feels like the most natural answer. Sometimes Taiwanese family roots matter more. Neither layer fully cancels the other.

That is why the conversation was meaningful to me. It gave me a neutral way to look back at my background without forcing it into one simple label.

title: Napier Seaside with My Mother
body: My mother used to drive me from Hastings or Havelock North to Napier, and somehow that seaside became attached to her.

title: Napier Seaside with My Mother (2)
body:
Napier seaside is one of the places I connect with my mother.

My mother lives around Hastings and Havelock North. When I went back to New Zealand, she would sometimes drive me to Napier. It was not a big trip, just a short drive to the seaside.

But I remember it quite clearly.

The car ride, the road, arriving near the water, walking for a while, maybe not even talking that much. It was ordinary, but it was one of the few things we repeatedly did together there.

My family’s connection with New Zealand is a little scattered. My sister studied there, my mother stayed there for her, and later that area became part of my mother’s own life too. For me, New Zealand was never only a travel place. It was also where part of my family was living without me.

So when my mother drove me to Napier, it felt like I was briefly entering that part of her life.

I do not remember Napier only as a beautiful seaside. I remember it as somewhere my mother brought me.

Maybe that is enough.

title: My Mother’s Cooking Across Places
body: A memory of my mother’s cooking, from home in Shanghai to later visits in New Zealand, and how food can carry the feeling of home across places.

title: My Mother’s Cooking Across Places (2)
body:
My mother has always been good at making food that is both healthy and actually good.

When I was younger and we were still living together in Shanghai, this felt very normal. She cooked at home, and I ate what she made. At that time, I probably did not think of it as something that needed to be remembered. It was just part of home.

Later, after life became more scattered and my mother spent more time in New Zealand, her cooking started to feel different to me. When I visited her there, having something she made was no longer just a normal meal. It became a small return to something familiar.

The food itself matters, of course. It is usually clean, warm, and carefully made. But what I remember more is the continuity. The same person who cooked for us in Shanghai was now cooking in another country, in another stage of life, and somehow the taste still carried something from before.

That is what makes it feel like home to me. Not because home is fixed in one city, or one apartment, or one period of life. Sometimes home appears through a repeated action: someone still cooking for you, still caring about whether you have eaten well, still making something ordinary but full of attention.

I do not get to eat my mother’s cooking all the time now. Maybe that is why, when I do, I notice it more.

title: The Sea Near My First Hong Kong Life
body: A memory of Kennedy Town seaside from my earlier HKU years, when the sea was close enough to become part of ordinary student life.

title: The Sea Near My First Hong Kong Life (2)
body:
I have not gone to Kennedy Town seaside much recently.

That feels a little strange, because in my first years at HKU, especially when I still lived closer to that side, the sea felt much more reachable. It was not a travel destination. It was just somewhere nearby, somewhere I could go without making it a big plan.

I do not remember every walk clearly now. A lot of those memories have probably become implicit. But I think that place still affected me. Being near the sea changed the feeling of the city a little.
