Hong Kong can feel dense, fast, and pressed together, but when I walked toward the water, the space suddenly opened.

Maybe part of that feeling also came from New Zealand, from the times my mother took me to the seaside there. The sea was already connected to something familiar before I came to Hong Kong. So Kennedy Town seaside was not only a new place. It also touched an older feeling.

What I liked was not a dramatic view. It was the movement of the water, the irregular waves, the space beyond the buildings, and the simple fact that the city could suddenly end in something wide.

Looking back, that seaside belongs to a particular stage of my Hong Kong life. I do not live that rhythm now, and I do not go there as often. But when I think of my early HKU years, the sea is still somewhere in the background.

title: Ferry Rides Across Sydney Harbour
body: A Sydney memory of taking public ferries across the harbour, moving between the Opera House, the bridge, quiet residential areas, commuters, and sunset.

title: Ferry Rides Across Sydney Harbour (2)
body:
One of my strongest memories of Sydney is not standing at one landmark, but moving through the harbour by ferry.

The ferries were not expensive, so we just kept taking them between different stops. That made the city feel less like a fixed map and more like something you could cross through from the water. Each route gave a slightly different version of Sydney.

Sometimes the ferry passed near the Opera House and the Harbour Bridge, and everything looked exactly as beautiful as people say. But other parts were different. Some areas felt more residential, with houses near the water that looked quiet, expensive, and almost unreal from the ferry.

I also remember one ride around the time people were getting off work. There were people in suits on the ferry, probably heading back home. When the ferry reached their stop, they got off in a very orderly way and walked toward those residential areas. For a moment it felt like I was seeing another person’s everyday life from the outside: work in the city, ferry across the water, home somewhere along the harbour.

The sunset made the whole thing even better. The bridge, the Opera House, the water, and the light all came together in a way that was almost too easy to remember.

Sydney stayed with me because of that movement. Not just one view, but the feeling of crossing between views, letting the city change slowly around the boat.

title: Gold Coast, Water, and the City Line
body: A Gold Coast memory of water activities, high views, and the strange beauty of a city stretched directly along the beach.

title: Gold Coast, Water, and the City Line (2)
body:
Gold Coast is one of the places where I remember the sea not only as something to look at, but as something I actually entered.

We did several water activities there. Kayaking, jet skiing, snorkelling, and the kind of activity where a boat pulls you up into the air above the water. I am not even fully sure what name I used for it at the time, but I remember the feeling clearly: the sea was not just background. It became part of the body, the speed, the wind, and the noise.

Another memory is from going up to one of those high observation decks. From above, the whole city looked like a line pressed against the coast: beach, water, high buildings, and long strips of sunlight all arranged together.

That view felt different from other cities I had seen. In many places, the sea and the city feel like separate things. But in Gold Coast, the buildings seemed to stand right beside the beach, almost as if the city had been drawn along the edge of the water.

I liked that contrast a lot. It was both urban and coastal, both energetic and holiday-like. You could be in a high-rise city, and at the same time feel that everything was still organized by the beach.

When I think of Gold Coast now, I think of water from two directions: being on it, and later looking down at it from high above.

title: A Road Trip Along the Great Ocean Road
body: A Melbourne exchange memory of friends renting a car and driving along the Great Ocean Road, where the journey mattered as much as the views.

title: A Road Trip Along the Great Ocean Road (2)
body:
Great Ocean Road is hard to write about because the first thing to say is too obvious: it was beautiful.

But what I remember more is that this was during my Melbourne exchange, and some friends rented a car and brought us along the road. Without them, it would probably have stayed as one of those places I knew I should visit. Because of them, it became part of my actual life there.

That day was not just about seeing the coastline. It was sitting in the car for hours with people who, a few months earlier, I did not even know. It was stopping wherever someone thought the view was worth it, taking photos, getting back in, talking, getting tired, and still wanting to see the next stop.

Exchange friendships are strange in that way. They form quickly, but they can carry a lot because everyone is living in a temporary period. You know it will not last in the same form, so the things you do together become heavier than they look.

The road itself kept changing: cliffs, ocean, open sky, small stops, famous viewpoints, and places I later put onto my website map. But I do not remember it as one clean scene. I remember it as movement, the car, the people, and the feeling that Melbourne was becoming more than a university term.

For me, Great Ocean Road is not only a scenic route. It is one of the moments when my exchange started to feel like a real lived period, because there were people beside me who made it feel that way.

title: A Birthday Somewhere Along Taiwan’s East Coast
body: A family road trip along Taiwan’s east coast, with my father driving, seaside cafés along the road, and one of my birthdays folded into the journey.

title: A Birthday Somewhere Along Taiwan’s East Coast (2)
body:
One of my happiest recent family memories is a road trip along Taiwan’s east coast.

It was the four of us: my father, my mother, my sister, and me. My father drove, and we moved along the eastern side of Taiwan, stopping in different places on the way. I do not remember every exact route clearly now, and maybe that is fine. What stayed with me is not the route as a travel record, but the feeling of being together in one car.

That kind of full family time has become rarer. Everyone is in different places, with different lives and schedules. So when we are all together for a trip, even normal things start to feel more memorable.

The east coast had a lot of sea. We would drive, see the water, stop somewhere, and sometimes find those small cafés near the coast. Many of them had their own style. We would sit down, drink coffee, look out at the sea, and then keep going.

There was nothing very dramatic about those stops, but that is why I liked them. They were not arranged to become important memories. They became important because they were simple, repeated, and shared.

My birthday also fell somewhere inside that trip. That made the whole journey feel even warmer to me. Not a big celebration in the formal sense, but a birthday inside a moving family trip, somewhere between the sea, the road, and coffee by the coast.

When I think back to it, I do not first remember a single landmark. I remember my father driving, the four of us together, and Taiwan’s east coast passing by outside the car window.

title: Athens, Ruins, and a Rare Family Trip
body: A family trip to Greece, where food, ruins, sunlight, and ancient stone made the place feel unlike anywhere I had been before.

title: Athens, Ruins, and a Rare Family Trip (2)
body:
Our family trip to Greece stayed with me partly because it was another rare period when the four of us could actually be together for a longer time.

We stayed for around two weeks, mostly around Athens, visiting different places, eating a lot, and moving through a city that felt very different from the places I was used to. Greece was not only pretty. It had a kind of historical weight that I had not really experienced before.

The ruins were the most obvious part of that. Places like the Acropolis made me feel that I was not just looking at an old building, but standing near something that belonged to a much longer line of human history. The stone, the columns, the open air, and the age of everything made the place feel almost unreal.

There was also one part my mother especially liked, with those carved figures standing like columns. I do not always remember the name immediately, but I remember the feeling of seeing them there, exposed to the sky, still holding that shape after so much time.

And then there was the food. I really liked Greek food: Greek salad, fried calamari, halloumi, and many other things that were simple but very satisfying. The food made the trip feel relaxed, not only grand or historical.

That combination is what I remember most: ancient places in the daytime, good food after walking, and my family being there together. Greece felt like a place where travel was not only about seeing something new, but about feeling that the world is much older and wider than my usual life.

title: Swimming in the Mediterranean
body: A Greece memory of taking a boat out to sea, swimming in the Mediterranean, and feeling the trip become physical rather than only visual.

title: Swimming in the Mediterranean (2)
body:
One of my clearest memories from Greece is swimming in the Mediterranean.

We went out by boat that day. I remember the sun, the water, the open sea around us, and my family nearby. It was not a day with much plot, but it stayed very clearly.

The strange thing is that the Mediterranean had always felt like a name to me before that. A sea on maps, in history, in photos of Greece. Then suddenly I was in it, not just looking at it from the coast.

I think that was my only time swimming there, which makes the memory feel more fixed. There are many beautiful views from that trip, but this one feels different because it was physical. The place was not only something I saw. It touched my body.

We spent time on the boat, rested in the sun, swam, and let the day pass slowly. Nothing needed to be explained.

When I think of Greece, I do not only think of ruins, streets, or blue views from far away. I also think of that day on the water, and the feeling of entering a sea I had known before only as a name.

title: Rainy Days as Permission to Stay In
body: A small daily feeling: rain makes staying indoors feel less like avoidance and more like something the world has quietly allowed.

title: Rainy Days as Permission to Stay In (2)
body:
I do not actually like rainy days that much.

Rain is inconvenient. The ground gets wet, the sky turns grey, and going anywhere becomes a little more annoying.

But I do like one thing about it: it gives me a reason not to go out.

On a sunny day, staying inside can feel like I am wasting the day. Even if nobody says anything, there is still a small pressure to do something, meet someone, walk somewhere, use the weather properly.

Rain removes that pressure.

It makes staying indoors feel less like a personal decision and more like the natural thing to do. The room becomes enough, not because I suddenly love being trapped inside, but because the outside has become less demanding.

So maybe I do not like rain itself.

I like the excuse it gives me.

title: The Mental Map of a Small Room
body: A small-room memory about mess, order, and why a space can feel familiar even when it does not look perfectly tidy from the outside.

title: The Mental Map of a Small Room (2)
body:
My mother sometimes says my room is messy.

She is probably not completely wrong. From the outside, it may look like things are placed everywhere. But to me, the room is not simply messy. I usually know where everything is. There is a map in my head.

That map is not always visible to other people. A book may be on one side, a cable somewhere else, a camera lens near the window, some small object on the desk. It may not look organized in the usual way, but I know the routes. I know where my hand should go when I need something.

This is why small rooms can feel quite intimate to me. In a small space, every object is close enough to become part of my mental map. The room may be crowded, but it is navigable. It has a kind of personal order that only makes full sense from inside my own habits.

A larger space can be more comfortable in an obvious way, but sometimes it also feels less close. Things are more spread out, and the relationship between me and the objects around me becomes less clear.

So when I say my room is not really messy, I do not mean it is objectively tidy. I mean it has a structure that I can feel. It is a little private, a little inefficient, maybe not very presentable, but still mine.

title: My First Camera and Another Way of Seeing
body: A memory of my Nikon Zfc, the apartment window, long lenses, travel photos, and how a camera gave me another way to look at the world.

title: My First Camera and Another Way of Seeing (2)
body:
My Nikon Zfc was my first real camera.

At the beginning, I did not even need to go far. I would stand in the apartment and shoot through the small window, trying different angles, looking for things outside that I would not normally pay attention to. After getting a longer lens, this became even more interesting. The lens could pull distant things closer and turn a random view outside the window into something worth looking at.

That was probably when photography started to change my relationship with going out. The camera gave me a reason to walk more, to look around more, and later to travel with a slightly different kind of attention. I was not only going somewhere. I was also looking for frames.

My mother has said before that people should use their eyes to see the world, not only keep taking photos. I actually think she is right. There are things the eyes experience directly that a photo cannot replace: the scale of a place, the air, the movement, the feeling of being there.

But I also think the camera gives another kind of seeing. It does not simply copy the eyes. It cuts the world differently. It notices details I might have passed over. It lets me return to a moment later and see it again from a distance.

Sometimes the photo is not even valuable because it is beautiful. It is valuable because it proves that I once looked at the world from that exact angle.

That is what the camera gave me: not a replacement for experience, but another layer of it.

title: Music That Became More Spacious
body: A small reflection on how my music taste moved from stronger beats and rap-like rhythm toward slower, wider, more atmospheric sound.

title: Music That Became More Spacious (2)
body:
My music taste has changed quite a bit.

When I was in high school, I was more drawn to music with stronger rhythm: EDM, rap, beats that moved quickly and gave a direct kind of energy. I still understand why I liked it. That kind of music pushes you forward. It is useful when you want intensity, speed, or just a clear hit of momentum.

Now I find myself liking music that feels more spacious.

Not necessarily music without rhythm. Rhythm still matters. But I am more attracted to something slower, wider, and more atmospheric. Sometimes it is pure instrumental music. Sometimes it still has lyrics, like Lana Del Rey's Young and Beautiful, but what I like is not only the words. It is the whole feeling behind it: the slow movement, the large background, the sense that the song opens up a space rather than just driving a beat.

I do not think this means my old taste was wrong. It is more like different stages of life need different sound structures. EDM still works for exercise because the body wants energy and repetition. But when I am not exercising, I often want music that gives me more room to think or feel.

Maybe that is the change. I used to want music to move me forward. Now I also want music to give me space.
